# Representations and World Models {#sec-data-representations-world-models}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The limits of my language mean the limits of my world."*
>
> — Ludwig Wittgenstein, *Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus* (1922)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** The philosopher Ludwig Wittgenstein argued that language constrains what we can conceive. The architectural version is narrower. A loop can only optimize and audit the state its representations, tools, and rejection rules expose. If an HDL has no field for a design concept, a generator may still mention it, but the loop cannot test or own it until the representation is extended.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Representation is one of the first hard problems in Architecture 2.0. Automated methods cannot act credibly unless architecture work is made legible. A bounded architecture study's representation must connect versioned design state, actions, observations at several fidelities, failed runs, rejected alternatives, and architect decisions. A world model adds a scoped prediction or constraint over that represented state. Together, they make architecture work comparable, revisable, and auditable, while a runnable receipt can additionally bind the record for replay.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What must a bounded architecture study record before an AI-assisted method can
act and a reviewer can audit the resulting architectural claim?
:::

@sec-architecture-20-ontology closed on the design-loop card. This chapter
opens its representation field and the world model attached to it. It examines
what state the loop records, what consequences
it predicts, and which assumptions stay visible for rejection. That ordering is
deliberate. It is tempting to begin Architecture 2.0 by asking which model, agent
framework[^fn-sw-agent-framework], or optimization method to use. But a method can only act on what the
loop can represent. *If the relevant state is invisible, a more capable model may simply move faster in the wrong direction.*

[^fn-sw-agent-framework]: An agent framework is a software structure defining how an autonomous system perceives its environment, reasons, and acts to achieve goals. No framework can act on state the representation never recorded, so its choice is secondary to designing the loop state it reads.

The scarce resource is not examples in the abstract, but paired records that
bind design intent to the actions tried, the observations returned, and the
decisions that followed, across versions and fidelity levels.
Architecture does not need data in the same way a large language model trained
on web text[^fn-ml-language-model-data-contrast] needs text.
The durable question is therefore not only how to collect more architecture
data. It is how to represent scarce architecture data so that a loop can act on
it and a human can audit it.
The architectural risk is not just small data; it is that a generative method
trained on public text may act as if it knew state that was never recorded.

[^fn-ml-language-model-data-contrast]: The relevant contrast is data shape. A web language model can learn from abundant public text; an architecture loop needs recorded state, provenance, tool outcomes, failed runs, and rejected alternatives that final prose usually omits.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can turn an architecture prompt, result, or artifact
into an actionable loop-state record for automation. That means you can:

- **Explain** why architecture data is not web data, because it is sparse, tool-bound, and full of state the final paper omits;
- **Treat** a feedback event as a sample that carries cost, fidelity, and provenance;
- **Spot** undocumented design state, missing failed runs, and missing rejected alternatives in an AI-assisted loop;
- **Recognize** the paired, versioned, multi-fidelity trajectory records needed to connect a design action to its outcome;
- **Tell** a study's architecture representation apart from its world model.
:::

## Why Architecture Data Is Not Web Data

Architecture differs from the domains that thrived on scraped data. Many successful AI systems benefit from abundant public text, images, code,
logs, or interaction traces. Architecture work is different. Some useful
material is public: papers, textbooks, manuals, open-source tools, benchmark
descriptions, and selected design artifacts. But much of the state that makes
an architecture decision meaningful is not public, not standardized, and not
preserved in the final paper. Some of it is tacit knowledge. It includes the design-review
habit that recognizes a fragile assumption, the instinct that a simulator
result is outside its calibrated regime, the memory of why a workload slice was
excluded, or the experience that a supposedly local change will become a
verification problem later.

ImageNet, a massive benchmark dataset for object recognition, is the useful foil. Its value was not that images happened to exist on the web. The challenge made a large public image collection comparable through
curation, labels, tasks, splits, and shared evaluation rules
[@RussakovskyEtAl2015ImageNet]. Architecture does have public text and some
public artifacts, but it rarely has the matching record of design intent, tool
state, failed candidates, and review decisions that would let a loop learn what
an architect actually learned.

The missing state matters. Workload traces may be proprietary or too large to
share. Simulator configurations may live in scripts rather than in the paper.
EDA reports may be confidential or tied to licensed process assumptions.
Labels may require expert judgment. Negative results are rarely published.
High-fidelity measurements may be slow, expensive, or unavailable until late
in the design process. The cost of a wrong action is also different. A weak
answer in a question-answering task may be corrected immediately. A weak
architecture proposal can consume weeks of simulation, mislead a design
review, or push effort toward a candidate that cannot survive synthesis,
timing, power, or software integration.

Benchmark practice shows what partial disclosure can buy. Standardized
benchmark suites such as SPEC CPU and MLPerf make a number meaningful only
because workload versions, run rules, system configuration, and reporting
conventions travel with the score [@SPEC2017CPU; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf].
Architecture loop data needs the same habit for candidate provenance, simulator
settings, excluded workloads, and rejected alternatives.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="The AI prompt is not the loop state"}
**Context.** This chapter's representation question is visible in the first noun
phrase of the lighthouse prompt. A user sentence can name a workload and technology
target, but it does not yet represent the design state the loop can act on.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** "64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem" and
"XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload" cannot be answered from public text
alone. XRBench gives a workload anchor [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench], and RISC-V
(@sec-architecture-20-ontology) gives the software-contract anchor.

**Representation.** The AI-assisted loop must also record the scenario, input distribution,
frame deadline, candidate compute organization, SoC boundary, software stack,
ISA and ABI assumptions, compiler choices, memory behavior, power model, process
assumptions, verification and reliability status, deployment context, and rejected
alternatives.

**Takeaway.** Treat the user's prompt as an index into missing loop state. An
automated design-space report is credible only when the representation carries enough
provenance, coverage, failed runs, and rejected alternatives for a human architect to audit.
:::

This is why internet-scale recipes transfer poorly if they are used naively.
Architecture data is sparse, expensive, tool-bound, and full of hidden
constraints, a contrast @tbl-web-vs-arch-data makes explicit.

| **Dimension** | **Web Data (Internet-Scale AI)** | **Architecture Data (Architecture 2.0)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Abundance | Hundreds of trillions of tokens. | Sparse; bound by slow simulation and engineering time. |
| Format | Mostly unstructured text, code, and images. | Heavily state-based, including tool flags, workloads, constraints, failed runs, and rejected alternatives. |
| Visibility | Publicly accessible (scraped corpora). | Often proprietary, tacit, or lost after publication. |
| Feedback Cost | Cheap (e.g., fast automated grading or immediate user correction). | Expensive (hours/days of cycle-level simulation or physical synthesis). |
| Cost of Failure | Low (a poor chat response is easily ignored). | High (a poor design choice wastes compute budgets or fails timing). |

: **Architecture data violates common web-corpus assumptions.** Recipes built for abundant public corpora and cheap proxy feedback transfer poorly when treated as sufficient for architecture loops, because architecture evidence is sparse, tool-bound, costly, and often private. {#tbl-web-vs-arch-data tbl-colwidths="[20,40,40]"}

The right response is not despair. It is representation design. Make the state
explicit enough that methods can act within the boundaries of what is known,
what is assumed, and what must still be checked. The representation records that
state and its history. A world model predicts or constrains what an action would
do.

> **Architecture representation.** An architecture representation is the
> bounded architecture study's implementation-facing record of what a method
> can read, compare, and update. The design-loop card points to this record
> rather than containing it. An implementation may expose three views of that
> one representation field:
>
> - **Current-state view:** Workload, design state, constraints, and the fields available at a particular version of the loop.
> - **Trajectory view:** Actions, observations, provenance, failed runs, rejected alternatives, and decisions that explain how the current state was reached.
> - **Context view:** Assumptions, uncertainty, tool versions, and other boundary conditions needed to interpret the first two views.

This representation records what the loop knows, but the loop also needs a mechanism to predict the outcome of its actions.

> **Architecture world model.** An architecture world model adapts the
> world-model idea from reinforcement learning [@HaSchmidhuber2018WorldModels]
> into a scoped belief model for a bounded design loop. It estimates or
> constrains what a candidate
> action would change, through a simulator, surrogate, rule system, or constraint
> model, and reports uncertainty and conditions it cannot evaluate. Human review
> remains outside the world model.

The boundary test in @tbl-representation-world-model-boundary is simple. A
representation records the state a loop can read. A world model predicts or
constrains the consequences of acting on the represented state. The same
artifact can contribute to more than one role, but the loop must say which role
it is playing. A runnable receipt is not a third role; it is a replay binding
on the representation side, tying recorded state to runnable inputs,
environment, provenance, and expected observations.

| **Example** | **Representation role** | **World-model role** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Simulator configuration | Records flags, model version, workload slice, seed, and command. | Defines what behavior the simulator can predict and where its calibration fails. |
| Candidate parameter table | Records legal choices and candidate values. | Feeds a surrogate or rule system that predicts latency, energy, area, or invalidity. |
| Constraint file | Records declared limits and assumptions. | Rejects actions that violate timing, power, interface, or policy boundaries. |
| Failed run log | Records what happened, with provenance. | Updates the loop's belief about invalid regions and escalation rules. |

: **Representation records state; world models predict consequences.** The loop needs both, and it must not confuse replayable artifacts with evidence that an action will remain valid under new workloads, tools, or fidelity levels. {#tbl-representation-world-model-boundary tbl-colwidths="[20,35,35]"}

The token count makes the web-versus-architecture contrast sharper. A back-of-the-envelope check across roughly fifty years of the literature puts public computer-architecture text on the order of $10^9$ tokens, at about 100,000 papers of 10,000 tokens each:
$$
T_{\mathrm{arch\text{-}text}}
\approx
N_{\mathrm{artifacts}} \times \bar{t}_{\mathrm{artifact}}
\approx
10^5 \times 10^4
\approx 10^9 \ \mathrm{tokens}.
$$
That sounds large, but it is small by web-scale pretraining[^fn-ml-pretraining] standards.
Worse, it is incomplete in the wrong way. Papers preserve accepted
claims far better than failed
configurations, simulator flags, workload revisions, EDA reports, review
arguments, and rejected alternatives. Architecture 2.0 therefore cannot treat
"all architecture text" as the dataset. The dataset must include the loop
state that made the text credible, and it must expose enough tacit judgment to
make assumptions, exclusions, and rejection decisions inspectable.
The token count matters only because it limits what a generative method can infer about
action authority. Text may retrieve concepts, but it cannot recover which
candidate was legal, what feedback was observed, why alternatives were rejected,
or who accepted the risk.

[^fn-ml-pretraining]: Pretraining is the computationally intensive initial phase of training a large machine learning model on a massive, general dataset before it is fine-tuned for specific tasks. It matters here because architecture's billion-token corpus is tiny next to the web-scale corpora that make pretraining effective, so the field cannot simply reuse the scrape-everything recipe.

The exact token count is not the main claim, as @tbl-architecture-data-scarcity
shows. Each row pairs a data layer's public visibility with the loop state it
still fails to carry, and the important scarcity is that the most useful architecture data are
often not public, not textual, or not preserved as reusable loop state.

| **Data layer** | **Rough public visibility** | **What is missing for Architecture 2.0** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Published papers, manuals, and tutorials | Public and mostly textual. | Failed runs, rejected alternatives, exact workload slices, scripts, seeds, and review rationale. |
| Open-source RTL, simulators, compilers, and benchmarks | Public but heterogeneous and version-sensitive. | Tool settings, run provenance, invalid configurations, and cross-tool disagreement. |
| Commercial EDA reports, PDK assumptions, and signoff context | Often private or redacted. | Timing, power, physical, waiver, and closure evidence tied to the claim. |
| Design reviews and engineer memory | Usually tacit. | Why an experiment was abandoned, which proxy was distrusted, and who accepted risk. |
| Paired design trajectories | Rarely public as connected, versioned records. | The state before an action, the action taken, observations at each fidelity, the rejection or acceptance, and the architect's decision. |

: **Architecture data scarcity is about missing design trajectories, not only token count.** Public text is useful, but the decisive records connect versioned state, actions, observations, provenance, rejected alternatives, and decisions across fidelity levels. {#tbl-architecture-data-scarcity tbl-colwidths="[26,28,36]"}

The data gap is therefore concrete. Architecture needs paired, versioned,
multi-fidelity trajectories rather than collections of final artifacts alone.
Each trajectory connects the state before an action, the action itself, the tool
or human observation, the fidelity and provenance of that observation, the
resulting rejection or acceptance, and the decision that changed the next
state (@fig-trajectory-record). Without those links, a dataset may show what
survived without showing what the design process learned.

![**A paired, versioned, multi-fidelity trajectory keeps rejected work linked to the state that produced it:** Each turn binds the design state before an action, the action itself, the observation with its cost, fidelity, and provenance, and the disposition and decision that produced the next version. The rejected candidate is not discarded; its failure record stays attached to the same state and action, so the boundary it exposed remains part of the record the next turn can read.](images/F5a-trajectory-record){#fig-trajectory-record width="100%" fig-alt="Two-turn trajectory diagram. From design state version one, an accepted action leads through an observation tagged with cost, fidelity, and provenance to a decision that produces state version two, while a second action from the same state leads to a rejected candidate whose failure record stays linked. A second turn repeats the chain from state version two to state version three."}

To visualize just how small the public-text estimate is, @fig-token-scale-contrast puts the architecture token count on the same log-scale
axis as three public AI-data anchors: the 1.4 trillion tokens used to train
DeepMind's Chinchilla model, Meta's report that Llama 3.1 405B was trained on more than 15 trillion
tokens, and a public human-text-stock estimate on the order of hundreds of
trillions of tokens
[@HoffmannEtAl2022Chinchilla; @MetaAI2024Llama31; @VillalobosEtAl2024DataLimits].
The point is not that architecture should race to scrape the web. The point is
that even a generous public architecture-text estimate sits more than three
orders of magnitude below a single 2022 training run, and more than five below
the estimated public human-text stock, while the most important missing records
are not ordinary text at all.

```{python}
#| label: fig-token-scale-contrast
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Architecture text is small by web-scale standards:** A roughly $10^9$-token public architecture-text estimate placed on the same log axis as three public anchors, the 1.4T tokens of Chinchilla's training run, the more than 15T pretraining tokens Meta reports for Llama 3.1 405B, and a roughly 400T public human-text stock estimate, with all values as reported by their sources. What architecture lacks is not a larger text collection but linked, versioned study trajectories.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Scale comparison plot showing that public architecture text is small relative to web-scale language-model corpora, while linked architecture-study trajectories are often missing."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, row_axis, top_log_axis

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter4-architecture-data-receipt.csv
# Stated values transcribed from sources, not recomputed here: Chinchilla 1.4T
# training tokens [@HoffmannEtAl2022Chinchilla]; Llama 3.1 405B reported as
# over 15T pretraining tokens [@MetaAI2024Llama31]; ~400T public human text
# stock scale anchor [@VillalobosEtAl2024DataLimits]. The 1B architecture-text
# figure is this book's own back-of-envelope check (10^5 artifacts x 10^4
# tokens per artifact). As-of: 2026-07-13.
rows = [
    {"display_label": "public architecture text estimate", "display_note": r"100k artifacts $\times$ 10k tokens", "right_label": "1B", "tokens": 1_000_000_000, "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "Chinchilla training run", "display_note": "Hoffmann et al. 2022", "right_label": "1.4T", "tokens": 1_400_000_000_000, "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "Llama 3.1 405B pretraining", "display_note": "Meta reports over 15T tokens", "right_label": ">15T", "tokens": 15_000_000_000_000, "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "public human text stock", "display_note": "Villalobos et al. 2024 scale anchor", "right_label": "~400T", "tokens": 400_000_000_000_000, "color": COLORS["purple"]},
]

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.35))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.43, right=0.86, top=0.78, bottom=0.30)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(1e8, 1e15),
    xticks=[1e8, 1e9, 1e12, 1e15],
    xticklabels=[r"$10^8$", r"$10^9$", r"$10^{12}$", r"$10^{15}$"],
    xlabel="log-scale token count",
    tick_fontsize=6.5,
)

for y, row in enumerate(rows):
    token_count = row["tokens"]
    color = row["color"]
    ax.axhline(y, color=COLORS["row"], linewidth=0.7, zorder=0)
    ax.hlines(y, 1e8, token_count, color=color, linewidth=2.2, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter([token_count], [y], marker="s", s=18, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], edgecolor=color, linewidth=0.9, zorder=3)
    ax.text(-0.97, y - 0.17, row["display_label"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.8, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(-0.97, y + 0.25, row["display_note"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=5.5, color=COLORS["muted"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(1.04, y, row["right_label"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.8, fontweight="bold", color=color, clip_on=False)

add_note_box(fig, "The missing architecture data are linked study trajectories,\nrather than a larger public text collection.", xywh=(0.09, 0.055, 0.82, 0.13), fontsize=6.3)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The assumption table in @tbl-architecture-data-receipt is a scale check that separates the easy
counts from the hard missing records. The multiplication that gives $10^9$
tokens is deliberately simple, and the public surfaces we can count most easily are not the
same thing as architecture loop state.
Treat the counts themselves as a July 2026 release snapshot, not a durable
benchmark.

| **Input** | **July 2026 release snapshot** | **What it supports** | **Caveat** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Public architecture, systems, and EDA artifacts** | $10^5$ paper-equivalent artifacts | Order-of-magnitude basis for the $10^9$-token scale check. | Assumption for intuition; not a measured corpus boundary. |
| **Tokens per paper-equivalent artifact** | $10^4$ tokens per artifact | Transparent multiplication: $10^5 \times 10^4 = 10^9$. | Tokenization and artifact length vary; manuals and specifications can be much larger. |
| **DBLP title pilot** | 1,015 title records from selected years of major architecture conferences (ISCA, MICRO, HPCA, and ASPLOS) | Shows that public metadata is easy to collect and useful for trajectory signals. | Title-only pilot; not full text, artifacts, tool state, or design-loop evidence. |
| **Missing loop-state records** | Not counted | The highest-value architecture data are traces, configs, logs, reports, reviews, negative results, and rejected candidates. | Much of this state is private, tacit, uncodified, or discarded before publication. |

: **The architecture-data scale check is an assumption log, not a corpus claim.** The current $10^9$-token estimate comes from transparent back-of-the-envelope assumptions, and the DBLP pilot is a metadata proxy, not a measured corpus boundary. {#tbl-architecture-data-receipt tbl-colwidths="[23,19,26,22]"}

The useful lesson from the scale check is the mismatch. Titles pulled from DBLP
(the public computer-science bibliography) and repository counts
can map a public surface, but they cannot recover simulator flags, failed
configurations, workload revisions, or review arguments. The repository-count
snapshots, and the questions of mining and maintaining that public surface,
belong in @sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources. The representation
requirement here is narrower. Public artifacts become useful loop data only
when they can be connected into paired design trajectories that carry
provenance, rejected alternatives, and decisions.

Results from other domains reinforce the distinction without changing it.
BioBERT, ClinicalBERT, and Med-BERT adapted models to biomedical text, clinical
notes, and structured health records
[@LeeEtAl2020BioBERT; @HuangEtAl2019ClinicalBERT; @RasmyEtAl2021MedBERT].
ChipNeMo similarly adapted language models to hardware-design text for
question-answering, script drafting, and bug-report summarization
[@LiuEtAl2023ChipNeMo]. SQuAD and GLUE showed how shared, scoreable examples can
organize evaluation [@RajpurkarEtAl2016SQuAD; @WangEtAl2018GLUE]. These examples
show that domain format matters. They do not supply the paired tool and decision
records an architecture loop needs. Text adaptation can improve access to
knowledge, but it does not reconstruct a multi-fidelity design trajectory.

## Sample Cost Belongs in the Trajectory {#sec-sample-cost-is-architecture-data}

Feedback cost changes what a sample is. In many benchmark settings, a sample is treated merely as an input-output pair, such as a
question and answer, an image and label, or a prompt and reference response. In an
architecture design loop, a sample is one observation event within a design
trajectory. It might be a cycle-level simulation, a
compiler report, a failed synthesis run, a power estimate, a rejected
floorplan, an expert review, or a silicon measurement. Each event has a cost,
fidelity, and provenance. Its value comes from being connected to the state and
action that produced it and to the rejection, acceptance, or revision that
followed.

Each recorded observation should therefore bind the prior state, action,
observation, cost, fidelity, provenance, coverage, disposition, and resulting
decision. None of this is a form an engineer fills in after the fact. In a
well-built loop the environment records these fields at run time, as a side
effect of executing the action
(@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces); the representation question
is what the harness must capture, not who does the paperwork.

The record must therefore preserve the burden and context of the observation,
not only its value. A
simulator point may require setup time, tool availability, license access,
calibration work, human triage, provenance, coverage context, and the
opportunity cost of not evaluating another candidate. A post-layout result may
carry higher fidelity but also higher latency and risk. A field deployment
measurement may be authoritative for one population and irrelevant for another.
These observations become a multi-fidelity trajectory only when the record also
links each one to the candidate version it evaluated and the next decision it
changed.

Concrete architecture tools span this range. Analytical mapping and dataflow
models are designed for broad design-space exploration
[@ParasharEtAl2019Timeloop; @KwonEtAl2019MAESTRO]. Physical-design and
verification flows expose the other end of the spectrum, where feedback can
take hours or days and the human and engineering cost becomes part of the
sample itself
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign; @BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing; @Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy].
@tbl-sample-cost-regimes is therefore a representation checklist. If the row
changes, the loop must record different state.

| **Feedback source** | **Latency / cost intuition** | **What it exposes** | **Record for reuse** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Analytical model or mapper | Milliseconds to seconds; low direct cost; high model-risk exposure. | Useful for pruning and sensitivity checks, not final evidence. | Model assumptions, workload slice, constraints, and proxy-validity notes. |
| Trace, profile, or replay | Seconds to hours depending on capture and replay setup. | Workload provenance is part of the sample. | Trace version, sampling policy, software stack, and filtering choices. |
| Cycle-level simulation | Minutes to days depending on model detail and target workload. | Simulator evidence is scoped by abstraction, calibration, and unsupported states. | Simulator version, configuration, seeds, workload revision, and calibration notes. |
| RTL, gate, or EDA feedback | Hours to days when synthesis, timing closure, power analysis, or physical DRC/LVS feedback enters the loop. | High-fidelity samples are scarce and multiobjective. | Tool versions, constraints, process assumptions, waived warnings, and rejected candidates. |
| FPGA, emulation, or prototype | High setup and shared-resource cost; high throughput once mapped. | Speed changes observability and debugging semantics, not only wall-clock time. | Mapping constraints, observability limits, debug hooks, and queue/resource state. |
| Post-silicon bring-up or validation | Weeks to months and high acquisition cost. | Real hardware exposes integration, timing, power, reliability, and validation failures that earlier evidence missed. | Hardware stepping, firmware or microcode version, test context, failure mode, waiver, and owner. |
| Field or fleet telemetry | Months to years and operationally confounded. | Real deployment shows workload drift, cohort effects, incidents, rollback triggers, and user-visible regressions. | Population, deployment version, sampling policy, privacy filter, incident context, and decision owner. |

: **Feedback events carry cost, fidelity, and decision scope.** A feedback event is useful only when the representation records what it cost, what assumptions it used, and what future decision it can support or reject. {#tbl-sample-cost-regimes tbl-colwidths="[20,24,24,22]"}

For tools like simulators, the timing side of this cost is multiplicative. A simulator is not slow in the abstract; it
is slow relative to the target cycles the workload demands. For a target clock
$f_{\mathrm{target}}$, workload duration $T_{\mathrm{workload}}$, and
simulation throughput $R_{\mathrm{sim}}$, the wall time is
$$
T_{\mathrm{wall}} =
\frac{N_{\mathrm{cycles}}}{R_{\mathrm{sim}}}
= \frac{f_{\mathrm{target}} \times T_{\mathrm{workload}}}{R_{\mathrm{sim}}}.
$$
For a 1 GHz target, the multiplication turns into wall-clock intuition in
@tbl-simulation-time. Read each row as a span of target execution time, each column as a
simulator throughput, and each cell as the wall-clock time to simulate that
span. One second of a 1 GHz target takes about 11.6 days at 1 kcycle/s but only
10 s at 100 Mcycle/s. The rates are illustrative, but the multiplication is the
point. For example, evaluating a cache prefetcher on a 10-second database query
trace would take about 116 days at the illustrative 1 kcycle/s rate. An
automated optimizer therefore cannot search a large parameter space at that
fidelity; it must first use a faster proxy and then escalate selected
candidates. A loop can afford many cheap proxy samples, fewer cycle-level
samples, and very few high-fidelity samples unless it has a disciplined plan
for escalation and rejection.

```{python}
#| output: asis
#| echo: false
import math

def human_time(s):
    if s < 60:
        return f"{s:g} s"
    m = s / 60
    if m < 60:
        return f"{m:.1f}".rstrip("0").rstrip(".") + " min"
    h = s / 3600
    if h < 24:
        return f"{h:.1f}".rstrip("0").rstrip(".") + " h"
    d = s / 86400
    if d < 365:
        return f"{d:.1f}".rstrip("0").rstrip(".") + " days"
    y = d / 365
    return (f"{y:.1f}".rstrip("0").rstrip(".") if y < 10 else f"{y:.0f}") + " years"

def sci(x):
    e = int(math.floor(math.log10(x)))
    m = x / 10 ** e
    return f"$10^{{{e}}}$" if abs(m - 1) < 0.05 else f"${m:g}\\times 10^{{{e}}}$"

freq = 1e9
workloads = [("1 ms", 1e-3), ("1 s", 1.0), ("1 min", 60.0), ("1 h", 3600.0)]
rates = [("1 kcycle/s", 1e3), ("100 kcycle/s", 1e5), ("10 Mcycle/s", 1e7), ("100 Mcycle/s", 1e8)]

rows = [
    "| **Target workload at 1 GHz** | **Target cycles** | " + " | ".join(f"**{r[0]}**" for r in rates) + " |",
    "|:--|:--|" + "|".join([":--:"] * len(rates)) + "|",
]
for wname, dur in workloads:
    cyc = freq * dur
    cells = " | ".join(human_time(cyc / rate) for _, rate in rates)
    rows.append(f"| {wname} | {sci(cyc)} | {cells} |")
rows.append("")
rows.append(
    ": **Target cycles turn simulator throughput into wall-clock pressure.** Even "
    "simple workloads become expensive when target execution time is multiplied by "
    "target frequency and divided by simulation throughput. Wall-clock values are "
    "computed from the cycle count and rate. {#tbl-simulation-time}"
)
print("\n".join(rows))
```

Plotted on log-log axes, the same multiplication becomes four parallel cost
lines, one per throughput, and a shaded one-day band shows how little of the
table a single working day can buy (@fig-simulation-wallclock). At the
illustrative 1 kcycle/s rate only the 1 ms workload finishes within a day; at
100 Mcycle/s even the one-hour workload still fits.

```{python}
#| label: fig-simulation-wallclock
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **A one-day budget cuts across the wall-clock lines:** Each line plots the illustrative wall-clock time $T_{\mathrm{wall}} = f_{\mathrm{target}} \times T_{\mathrm{workload}} / R_{\mathrm{sim}}$ for one simulator throughput at a 1 GHz target, and the markers are the sixteen cells of @tbl-simulation-time. The shaded band marks runs that finish within one day. A faster simulator shifts its whole line down by a constant factor, so the ways to keep a workload inside the band are higher throughput, a shorter workload slice, or accepting fewer high-fidelity samples.
#| out-width: "86%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-log chart of wall-clock simulation time versus target execution time at 1 gigahertz, with four parallel lines for simulator throughputs from 1 kilocycle per second to 100 megacycles per second and a shaded band marking runs that finish within one day. At 1 kilocycle per second only the 1 millisecond workload stays inside the band, at about 17 minutes, while one second of target time takes 11.6 days; at 100 megacycles per second even the one-hour workload finishes in about 10 hours."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

# Same illustrative computation as @tbl-simulation-time: wall time =
# (f_target x T_workload) / R_sim for a 1 GHz target. No external dataset;
# every point is computed in-cell from the table's illustrative rates.
freq = 1e9
workloads = [("1 ms", 1e-3), ("1 s", 1.0), ("1 min", 60.0), ("1 h", 3600.0)]
rates = [
    ("1 kcycle/s", 1e3, COLORS["red"]),
    ("100 kcycle/s", 1e5, COLORS["orange"]),
    ("10 Mcycle/s", 1e7, COLORS["blue"]),
    ("100 Mcycle/s", 1e8, COLORS["green"]),
]
day = 86_400.0

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.6))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.15, right=0.78, top=0.95, bottom=0.17)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(5e-4, 7e3)
ax.set_ylim(2e-3, 3e10)

ax.axhspan(2e-3, day, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], zorder=0)
ax.axhline(day, color=COLORS["note_edge"], linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--", zorder=1)
ax.text(8e-4, day * 2.2, "one day", fontsize=5.8, color=COLORS["note_text"],
        fontweight="bold", va="bottom")
ax.text(2.6e-3, 4.5e-3, "finishes within one day", fontsize=5.8,
        color=COLORS["note_text"], fontweight="bold", va="bottom")

xs = [d for _, d in workloads]
for name, rate, color in rates:
    ys = [freq * d / rate for d in xs]
    ax.plot(xs, ys, color=color, linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=14, color=color, zorder=3)
    ax.text(3600 * 1.4, ys[-1], name, va="center", fontsize=6.0, color=color,
            clip_on=False)

ax.set_xticks([1e-3, 1.0, 60.0, 3600.0])
ax.set_xticklabels(["1 ms", "1 s", "1 min", "1 h"])
ax.set_yticks([1.0, 60.0, 3600.0, day, 3.156e7, 3.156e9])
ax.set_yticklabels(["1 s", "1 min", "1 h", "1 day", "1 year", "100 years"])
ax.minorticks_off()
ax.set_xlabel("target execution time at 1 GHz", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("wall-clock simulation time", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Stack a fixed evaluation budget against these per-sample costs and the
affordable counts collapse by almost seven orders of magnitude, from roughly
$10^8$ analytic evaluations to about ten prototype measurements in the same
1,000 CPU-days (@fig-affordable-sample-collapse).

```{python}
#| label: fig-affordable-sample-collapse
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **One evaluation budget buys wildly different sample counts across feedback regimes:** Under an illustrative fixed budget of 1,000 CPU-days, assumed per-sample costs of about 1 s for an analytic model, 2.8 h for a cycle-level run (one second of a 1 GHz target at the 100 kcycle/s rate in @tbl-simulation-time), 3 days for an RTL/EDA result, and 90 days for a prototype measurement collapse the affordable sample count by almost seven orders of magnitude. The rates are illustrative, matching @tbl-sample-cost-regimes; the collapse is the point, and it is why the loop needs a disciplined plan for escalation and rejection.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale row chart of affordable sample counts under a fixed 1,000 CPU-day budget: roughly ten to the eighth analytic-model evaluations, ten to the fourth cycle-level simulations, a few hundred RTL or EDA results, and about ten prototype measurements."

import math
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, row_axis, top_log_axis

# Illustrative budget-composition check. No external dataset: per-sample costs
# take one representative value from the in-chapter ranges (analytic
# "milliseconds to seconds" -> 1 s; cycle-level = 1 s of a 1 GHz target at
# 100 kcycle/s = 2.8 h from tbl-simulation-time; RTL/EDA "hours to days" ->
# 3 days; prototype/post-silicon "weeks to months" -> 90 days from
# tbl-sample-cost-regimes). Budget: 1,000 CPU-days.
budget_s = 1_000 * 86_400
rows = [
    {"display_label": "analytic model or mapper", "display_note": "~1 s per evaluation", "cost_s": 1.0, "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "cycle-level simulation", "display_note": "~2.8 h per run", "cost_s": 2.8 * 3600, "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "RTL, gate, or EDA feedback", "display_note": "~3 days per result", "cost_s": 3 * 86_400, "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "FPGA prototype or silicon", "display_note": "~90 days per measurement", "cost_s": 90 * 86_400, "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

def approx_label(n):
    if n >= 1000:
        return r"$\sim$10$^{%d}$" % round(math.log10(n))
    return rf"$\sim${round(n / 10) * 10 if n >= 30 else round(n)}"

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.35))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.43, right=0.86, top=0.78, bottom=0.30)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(1, 1e9),
    xticks=[1, 1e3, 1e6, 1e9],
    xticklabels=[r"$10^0$", r"$10^3$", r"$10^6$", r"$10^9$"],
    xlabel="affordable samples under a 1,000 CPU-day budget",
    tick_fontsize=6.5,
)

for y, row in enumerate(rows):
    count = budget_s / row["cost_s"]
    color = row["color"]
    ax.axhline(y, color=COLORS["row"], linewidth=0.7, zorder=0)
    ax.hlines(y, 1, count, color=color, linewidth=2.2, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter([count], [y], marker="s", s=18, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], edgecolor=color, linewidth=0.9, zorder=3)
    ax.text(-0.97, y - 0.17, row["display_label"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.8, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(-0.97, y + 0.25, row["display_note"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=5.5, color=COLORS["muted"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(1.04, y, approx_label(count), transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.8, fontweight="bold", color=color, clip_on=False)

add_note_box(fig, "The budget's composition is forced by arithmetic; escalation\nand rejection decide which few samples earn full fidelity.", xywh=(0.09, 0.055, 0.82, 0.13), fontsize=6.3)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

This cost structure changes how we should think about design spaces. A small
co-design exercise with five workload slices, four architecture configurations,
and six compiler or mapping settings has $5 \times 4 \times 6 = 120$
candidates and can sometimes be enumerated. A physical-design, compiler,
mapping, or chiplet-integration space may be combinatorial, sequential,
tool-bound, and partially invalid.
Learning-assisted chip placement makes the point concrete. The design problem
can be formulated as a learning problem, but the value of each sample depends
on the representation of macros, nets, constraints, tool feedback, and
placement validity [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]; the later reproducibility
dispute over that result (@sec-feedback-verification-trust)
[@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL] only sharpens the point. The architecture
lesson is broader than placement. When samples are expensive, the loop record
must capture what each sample cost, what region of the space it informs, what it
rules out, and how it should constrain the next optimizer action.

@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization returns to sample efficiency from the method side. The representation
lesson comes first. If cost, fidelity, and rejected-space coverage are not
recorded, a later optimizer cannot know whether it is learning the design
space or merely collecting disconnected measurements.

## Architecture Descriptions as Boundary Objects {#sec-architecture-descriptions-as-boundary-objects}

All of that recorded state has to live somewhere the loop and the architect can
both read, and that artifact, the architecture description, is a boundary
object. In the sense introduced by Star and Griesemer, a boundary object is
shared across communities while still supporting their different local uses
[@StarGriesemer1989BoundaryObjects]. It sits between human intent and tool action. It must be readable enough for architects to inspect, precise
enough for tools to execute, and structured enough for automated optimizers or methods to
modify without breaking the design contract.

At minimum, an architecture description should state what it describes, what
may change, what must remain fixed, what evidence may update the record, and
which tools may consume it. For a
memory hierarchy, this may include cache sizes, associativity, replacement
policy, prefetching, coherence assumptions, bandwidth, latency, and workload
mix. For an accelerator or compute subsystem, it may include supported
operations, data layout, local storage, vector width, dataflow, quantization,
compiler/runtime assumptions, and fallback behavior.
For an optimizer-facing architecture description, fields alone are not enough.
The representation must identify mutable fields, fixed constraints, and
observations that may update the record. The environment defined in
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces determines whether a proposed
action is legal and how violations are rejected or escalated.

The important point is not that every representation must be one universal
schema. Different loops need different representations. A paper-reading loop,
a simulator-driven design-space exploration loop, an RTL-generation loop, and
a post-silicon telemetry loop should not have identical records. But they do
need explicit invariants.

Without those boundaries, a representation becomes a prompt-shaped anecdote.
It may sound plausible, but it cannot safely drive action.

## Unstructured Design Data and Its Cost

When those boundaries are left implicit, the missing structure does not stay
free. Architecture teams accumulate undocumented design state, the same kind of
hidden cost that software teams call technical debt when complex data
dependencies and implicit assumptions go unmanaged [@SculleyEtAl2015Hidden].
The cost appears whenever
important design state exists but is not captured in a durable, inspectable
form. It may live in shell scripts, simulator flags, spreadsheet formulas,
plotting notebooks, benchmark directories, issue threads, email, review
slides, or the memory of the person who knows why one candidate was rejected.
Some of this state is tacit rather than textual, including what an experienced architect
chooses not to try, which proxy result they distrust, which corner case they
ask about in review, and which risk they refuse to delegate.

This gap is manageable when a small team manually coordinates the loop. It
becomes a technical failure when an AI system acts inside the loop. If a
constraint is implicit, the method may violate it. If a simulator flag is
hidden, the result may not be replayable. If rejected candidates are missing,
the search may rediscover known failures. If workload provenance is unclear,
the loop may optimize for the wrong distribution. If plots preserve only the
winning candidate, the evidence trail cannot explain why alternatives were
discarded.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The result whose data stayed closed"}
**The claim.** Consider AI-generated chip placement, the AlphaChip result this book follows. A reinforcement-learning method was reported to perform macro placement on production netlists as well as or better than human experts, and said to have run on production silicon [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement].

**The gap.** The designs the method learned from were proprietary, and while some code was later released, the pretraining inputs needed to rerun the strongest version of the claim were not [@ChengEtAl2023MacroPlacement; @Markov2023FalseDawn]. Because this proprietary pretraining data was kept closed, independent groups could not reproduce that crucial step, and the original authors answered that the critics had not run the method as described [@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed]. Whatever placement priors or transferable representation the method learned from those chips, its coverage, assumptions, and blind spots cannot be inspected, so a reviewer acting in good faith cannot audit the data behind the claim.

**The lesson.** From public artifacts alone, the strongest version of the claim cannot be fully confirmed or rejected, and the lesson is not who is right. Bounded public evaluations can still test released setups, baselines, and proxies; they just cannot replay the private production and pretraining context behind the strongest claim. Enforce provenance for data and environment as the run happens, so the loop leaves a record a reviewer can audit rather than a dispute.
:::

This missing information typically hides in common, recurring sources of undocumented
design state, which @tbl-representation-debt
collects; read each row from what the artifact enables to what it hides to
the failure that follows when a loop trusts it. The point is not to document everything for its own sake. The point is
to capture enough state that a loop can compare, reject, and revise, and that a
runnable receipt can bind to a replay.

| **Artifact** | **What it enables** | **What it often hides** | **Failure mode** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Paper or plot | Claim, result, and comparison. | Tool flags, failed candidates, tuning history. | Reproduce only the story, not the loop. |
| Workload trace | Concrete input behavior and measurements. | Coverage, versioning, sampling policy, privacy filters. | Optimize for an unrepresentative slice. |
| Simulator config | Explicit model setting. | Defaults, unsupported states, calibration limits. | Trust a number outside its valid scope. |
| RTL or EDA report | Implementation-facing feedback. | Process assumptions, constraints, waived warnings. | Accept an artifact that cannot close. |
| Review notes | Human judgment and rationale. | Tacit assumptions and discarded alternatives. | Lose why a decision was made. |
| Rejected candidate | Search boundary and negative evidence. | Why it failed and at what fidelity. | Rediscover known dead ends. |

: **Architecture artifacts become reusable loop data when they are representation records.** Each artifact should carry enough provenance, assumptions, constraints, and validity information for a loop to act on it and for a reviewer to audit it. {#tbl-representation-debt tbl-colwidths="[22,27,25,18]"}

## QuArch as a Stress Test

The artifacts in @tbl-representation-debt are internal and often unstructured.
Public benchmarks sit at the opposite extreme, well structured but paper-bound.
QuArch (@sec-architecture-20-ontology) is a useful stress test for this chapter
precisely because it exposes what that public structure still leaves out
[@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch]. It bridges two representation layers,
architecture knowledge that can be asked about in text and linked study
trajectories that must be recorded before an automated optimizer can act. The later QuArch reasoning benchmark makes the
same point more explicit by organizing 2,671 expert-validated questions around
recall, analysis, design, and implementation competencies
[@PrakashEtAl2025QuArchReasoning]. QuArch can ask whether a model recalls
concepts, tracks architectural relationships, reasons over published claims,
and avoids obvious domain mistakes. That is valuable. A field cannot build
credible AI-assisted systems if those systems lack basic architectural knowledge.

That achievement is necessary but not sufficient, as @fig-quarch-boundary-test
makes clear. Paper-derived questions test one layer of architecture data, while
tool use and claim review require linked study trajectories that papers often omit.

![**QuArch is a boundary test, not the whole data layer.** Question-answering benchmarks can test architectural knowledge from papers, but AI-assisted architecture also needs linked study trajectories that bind claim and scope, represented state and actions, tool outcomes, provenance, failed paths, review, and decision.](images/F5b-quarch-boundary-test){#fig-quarch-boundary-test width="100%" fig-alt="Two-panel diagram showing a paper-knowledge layer with concept recall, published relationships, and paper-level reasoning separated from a study-trajectory layer linking claim scope, state, actions, tool outcomes, provenance, failed paths, review, and decision."}

The boundary is precise. QuArch represents architectural knowledge from papers,
while a design trajectory represents what happened during an experiment. A
model may answer a memory-system question correctly and still lack the tool
configuration, workload slice, failed candidates, or review decision needed to
act on the claim. QuArch is therefore one valuable layer of the data picture,
not a substitute for recorded design trajectories.

## Toward Architecture World Models {#sec-toward-architecture-world-models}

QuArch tests what a model knows about architecture; a world model concerns what
the loop can predict. The earlier definition separated recorded state from
predicted consequence. The remaining question is how to attach predictions to
the trajectory without confusing them with observations. A simulator
configuration belongs to the representation. The simulator's modeled behavior,
scope, calibration, and failure modes form a world model. Design rules, expert
heuristics, learned surrogates, and physical constraints can play the same
predictive or constraint role. Learned models that predict performance from
configuration are not new; predictive design-space exploration built them two
decades ago [@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. What the world-model framing adds is
not the predictor but the contract around it. The model must declare its scope,
its calibration, and the conditions under which its prediction is unsupported.
This relationship, drawn in @fig-architecture-world-model, keeps the
observation returned by a tool distinct from the prediction that preceded it.

![**A world model connects represented state to predicted consequences:** A representation captures what the study can read, compare, and update; a world model predicts or constrains what an action will change within stated scope limits. Observations become reusable when provenance, coverage, failed runs, and rejected alternatives are recorded, while replay additionally requires runnable inputs and environment bindings.](images/F5-architecture-world-model){#fig-architecture-world-model width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram showing representation records, a scoped world model, tool observations, provenance, coverage, failed runs, and rejected alternatives connected to predicted architectural consequences, with replay requiring a separate runnable receipt."}

For the lighthouse prompt, a small world model might be as simple as
@tbl-xr-world-model-sketch. It is not a full simulator. It is the scoped model
that says which actions are meaningful, what they are expected to change, and
what evidence can overturn the prediction.

| **World-model field** | **XRBench/RISC-V sketch** |
| --- | --- |
| **State** | Workload slice, frame deadline, software path, candidate compute organization, memory traffic, and power envelope. |
| **Action under evaluation** | Change vector width, local memory size, CPU/accelerator partition, or data layout inside the declared software contract. |
| **Predicted transition** | Estimate latency, memory traffic, energy, area pressure, and compiler/runtime feasibility. |
| **Uncertainty and calibration** | State whether the estimate comes from an analytic proxy, calibrated simulator, prior measurements, or expert rule. |
| **Unsupported condition** | Mark predictions unsupported when a candidate breaks software compatibility, leaves the modeled action range, or lacks required state. |
| **Calibration check** | Request a stronger observation when a candidate nears the 3\ W target, misses the frame deadline, or wins only under a weak proxy. |

: **An architecture world model is a scoped belief about action and consequence.** Even a small sketch should name represented state, the action under evaluation, predicted outcomes, uncertainty, unsupported conditions, and the observation needed to check the prediction. {#tbl-xr-world-model-sketch tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

World models may be simulator-backed, learned, symbolic, or hybrid. What matters
here is that each predicts or constrains consequences only within a stated
scope. The environment in @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces will
define which actions are legal and which observations tools return. The world
model consumes those actions and predicts their consequences. It does not own
the action or observation contract.

No world model is automatically credible. Each has a scope. Each has
uncertainty. Each can be wrong under distribution shift[^fn-ml-distribution-shift], new workloads,
different software stacks, tool changes, or higher-fidelity evaluation. The
goal is not to pretend the world model is truth. The goal is to make its
assumptions explicit enough that the loop can decide when to trust it and when
to request a stronger observation. The lighthouse prompt makes that discipline
concrete. An analytic proxy predicts that a wider-vector
candidate meets the 3\ W envelope, but the margin to the envelope is smaller
than the proxy's known error band, so the calibration check from
@tbl-xr-world-model-sketch requests a simulator observation, while a narrower
candidate whose margin exceeds the error band is decided at proxy fidelity
without one (@fig-proxy-margin-escalation). The simulator shows the
wider-vector candidate misses the frame deadline on the real XRBench slice, and the architect
records the rejection with its fidelity level and reason. The prediction, the
calibration check, and the resulting observation are auditable, but they belong
to different parts of the loop. The prediction and calibration check belong to
the world model. The simulator result, rejected candidate, and architect
decision belong to the recorded trajectory, so a reviewer can see why the
earlier belief was overturned.

[^fn-ml-distribution-shift]: Distribution shift is a situation where the data a model meets in deployment differs significantly from the data it was trained on, often degrading performance [@QuinoneroCandelaEtAl2009DatasetShift]. It is one of the ways a world model can be wrong, which is why the loop records assumptions and calibration so it can tell when a prediction has left its supported regime.

```{python}
#| label: fig-proxy-margin-escalation
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Escalate when the margin to the envelope sits inside the proxy's error band:** Two illustrative candidates under an analytic power proxy with a $\pm 0.25$ W error band, judged against the lighthouse prompt's 3 W envelope. The narrower candidate's band clears the envelope, so it can be accepted or rejected at proxy fidelity; the wider-vector candidate's band straddles it, so the calibration check buys a simulator observation before any disposition. The values are constructed to draw the rule; the rule, not the numbers, is the durable part.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Interval chart of predicted power for two candidates against a 3-watt envelope line. A narrower candidate at about 2.4 watts has an error band clearing the envelope and is marked decide at proxy fidelity. A wider-vector candidate at about 2.9 watts has an error band straddling the envelope and is marked escalate to a simulator observation."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

# Illustrative values only. The 3 W envelope is the lighthouse prompt's power
# target; the proxy predictions (2.4 W, 2.9 W) and the +/-0.25 W error band
# are constructed to draw the calibration-check rule, not measured.
envelope_w = 3.0
band_w = 0.25
candidates = [
    {"label": "narrower candidate", "note": "margin exceeds error band", "verdict": "decide at\nproxy fidelity", "pred": 2.4, "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"label": "wider-vector candidate", "note": "margin inside error band", "verdict": "escalate to\nsimulator", "pred": 2.9, "color": COLORS["orange"]},
]

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 1.75))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.30, right=0.82, top=0.90, bottom=0.26)

ax.set_ylim(-0.6, len(candidates) - 0.4)
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlim(2.0, 3.45)
ax.set_xticks([2.0, 2.25, 2.5, 2.75, 3.0, 3.25])
ax.set_xlabel("proxy-predicted power (W)", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="x", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="x", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55)
for spine in ["top", "right", "left"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

ax.axvline(envelope_w, color=COLORS["red"], linewidth=1.2, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)
ax.text(envelope_w + 0.03, -0.52, "3 W envelope", fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["red"], ha="left", va="top", fontweight="bold")

for y, cand in enumerate(candidates):
    lo, hi = cand["pred"] - band_w, cand["pred"] + band_w
    ax.axhline(y, color=COLORS["row"], linewidth=0.65, zorder=0)
    ax.hlines(y, lo, hi, color=cand["color"], linewidth=2.2, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter([lo, hi], [y, y], marker="|", s=42, color=cand["color"], linewidth=1.4, zorder=3)
    ax.scatter([cand["pred"]], [y], marker="s", s=18, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], edgecolor=cand["color"], linewidth=0.9, zorder=3)
    ax.text(-0.03, y - 0.14, cand["label"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="right", va="center", fontsize=6.8, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(-0.03, y + 0.20, cand["note"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="right", va="center", fontsize=5.5, color=COLORS["muted"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(1.03, y, cand["verdict"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.2, fontweight="bold", color=cand["color"], clip_on=False, linespacing=1.25)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

## Provenance, Coverage, and Failed Alternatives {#sec-provenance-coverage-and-negative-traces}

Knowing when to reject the world model's advice depends on records most teams
are quick to discard. The most distinctive architecture data may be the data
the field usually throws away[^fn-systems-negative-results-venues]. Published work tends to preserve accepted artifacts. Design loops
also need rejected alternatives. A failed simulator run, invalid configuration,
proxy win that fails at higher fidelity, abandoned floorplan, or
unsupported-software path is not merely noise. It shows the loop where the
boundary lies.

Other engineering communities already treat some failures this way. A compiler
bug that becomes a regression test, or a security failure that becomes a
standing check, is no longer only a past mistake. It is a reusable boundary that
keeps later automation from rediscovering the same invalid region. Architecture
loops need the same habit, with enough provenance to know when the old failure
still applies.

[^fn-systems-negative-results-venues]: The wider computing-systems community has begun building places to publish exactly these records, so rigorous experiments that fail to confirm a hypothesis can still enter the shared record. Architecture needs more of that habit so failed runs, rejected candidates, and the assumptions behind them accumulate as shared evidence instead of disappearing before publication.

Failed runs and rejected alternatives are necessary because a loop that records
only winners cannot show the next method, reviewer, or architect which
assumptions, tools, workloads, or regions of the design space were already ruled
out. Architecture action spaces are full of invalid or misleading moves. A
generated RTL fragment may be syntactically plausible
but fail timing or violate an interface. A design-space search may find a
candidate that looks good under a proxy but fails under a better power model.
A benchmark result may improve because the workload slice is too narrow. A
chiplet partition may appear modular but introduce unacceptable latency,
thermal coupling, test complexity, or supply-chain risk.

The record in @tbl-negative-traces shows what failed, what boundary it
exposed, and how that evidence should change the next action in the loop.

| **Failed run or rejected alternative** | **What it records** | **What the loop learns** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Synthesis or timing violation | Constraints, process, parameters, failing setup/hold paths. | Exclude this macro or parameter combination. |
| Unroutable or PDN failing netlist | Placement, density, pin layout, congestion map, IR drop. | Add congestion-aware spacing or reshape the action space. |
| Proxy win that fails fidelity | Cheap metric improves, but stronger evaluation rejects it. | Calibrate proxies and require sensitivity checks. |
| Tool failure or crash | Simulator, synthesis, compiler, or harness cannot complete. | Separate design failure from environment failure. |
| Coverage gap | Workload, input, scenario, or architecture class was not represented. | Mark the evidence boundary before committing. |
| Rejected design rationale | Human review rejects a candidate for risk, maintainability, schedule, or integration. | Preserve architect judgment as training and audit data. |

: **Failed runs and rejected alternatives are architecture data.** Failed builds, invalid candidates, missed constraints, and rejected alternatives define the boundary of the design space and should be preserved rather than discarded. {#tbl-negative-traces tbl-colwidths="[26,32,32]"}

These records require enough provenance to interpret the environment that
produced the result. A replay claim additionally requires the runnable
environment and inputs. A failed result without context is not very useful. The
minimum reusable record names the candidate and its context (workload slice,
software stack, tool version, seed, parameters, and constraints), the fidelity
level, the failure reason, the coverage boundary, and the decision owner. The loop also
needs coverage. What part of the design space, workload space, or evidence
regime does this record represent? Without provenance and coverage, failed runs
become a pile of anecdotes. With them, they become architecture data.

A failure record also needs a reason to survive ordinary deadline pressure. It
should save a future run, justify a review decision, preserve a handoff, bound a
public claim, or mark the condition under which an old rejection should be
reopened. Otherwise failure records become either clutter or folklore, and the
loop cannot tell whether a past rejection is still valid.

## When a Representation Becomes Actionable

Collecting this architecture data serves a narrow goal, action and audit
rather than coverage. A representation becomes actionable when a method and a
reviewer can reconstruct what state was seen, what action was attempted, what
observation returned, why an alternative failed or was rejected, and how the
next state followed. That bar fixes the canonical content of the trajectory
record, the one list this chapter's earlier sections have been circling:
versioned workload and candidate state, constraints and assumptions, the
actions tried, the observations returned with their cost, fidelity,
provenance, and uncertainty, coverage, failed runs and rejected alternatives,
and the decision history. A runnable receipt adds the environment and inputs
needed for replay.

This is a higher bar than asking whether a model can read it. Four failure
patterns make that bar concrete:

- A representation that supports summarization may still omit the state a
  method needs in order to act.
- A representation that supports search may still hide which actions are
  invalid.
- A representation that stores scores may still omit their provenance.
- A representation that records winners but omits rejected alternatives may
  bias the loop's view of the design space.

The same standard applies to abstractions. A useful Architecture 2.0 abstraction
does not merely hide detail or provide nicer names. It preserves the state and
trajectory needed to connect a proposed change to a later observation. An
abstraction that loses that connection may help people talk, but it has not made
the loop more inspectable.

For the lighthouse prompt, an actionable representation would not merely
contain the prompt text. It would include the XRBench scenario, workload
metadata, architecture parameters, software assumptions, power and process
constraints, tool configurations, candidate versions, available evaluation
resources, observations at each fidelity, failed runs, rejected alternatives,
and the decisions that followed. That record makes the state legible. It does
not yet define which changes are legal or which observations a tool can return.
@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces owns that next interface.

::: {#pri-make-legible .callout-design-principle title="Make architecture work legible to the automated loop"}
A representation earns trust only when it records provenance, assumptions,
costs, failed runs, and rejected alternatives, not only the successful endpoints. What
the loop cannot see, it cannot reason about, and what the human architect cannot see, they cannot safely reject.
:::

## Open Research Questions

The gap between public architecture knowledge and actionable loop state is
significant. The questions here focus on representations and world models, not
yet on full tool environments or governance. Each question asks what structure,
learning problem, or benchmark would make architecture state rejectable and
useful inside an AI-assisted loop, and what a receipt must add for replay.

1. **What semantics make architecture proposals checkable and rejectable?**
Representations must act as boundary objects between human intent and tool
execution, as discussed in @sec-architecture-descriptions-as-boundary-objects.
Yet optimizer proposals are still often described informally. A thesis-level
contribution would define a machine-readable proposal language with operational
semantics for task intent, design-space boundaries, evidence, and rejection.
The evaluation should show that proposals can be checked, compared, and safely
rejected from attached records, then replayed when the runnable environment,
inputs, commands, expected observations, and provenance are also present.

2. **How should multi-fidelity design trajectories be recorded?**
Every simulation, synthesis run, trace collection, or physical-flow check is a
scarce measurement of the design space. The needed dataset connects each sample
to the prior state and action and preserves the full trajectory record for it,
from workload provenance and tool versions through disposition and resulting
decision. Its value should be tested
by whether it improves later prediction, prevents invalid transfer, or lets
reviewers understand why a claim is or is not supported. The question treats
sample cost as part of the trajectory, in the sense developed in @sec-sample-cost-is-architecture-data.

3. **When is an architecture world model outside calibrated support?**
The world model sketch in @tbl-xr-world-model-sketch uses an explicit
calibration check, but a stronger research result would learn or verify where
that boundary should be. A concrete paper could build a calibration-boundary model
that predicts when a proxy has left its support region and should route a
candidate to cycle-level simulation or physical synthesis. The success metric
is not prediction accuracy alone. It is lower evidence cost at the same
false-accept risk, or lower false-accept risk at the same evidence cost.

4. **What coverage record tells reviewers when a result generalizes?**
Architecture loops need records that describe which workloads, phases, trace
slices, objectives, constraints, and failure modes a representation has
actually seen. The research artifact is a coverage record for architecture
agents, tested on shifted-workload benchmarks. The goal is to tell reviewers
when a claimed design-space lesson transfers, when it weakens, and when it
should be rejected. This extends the generalization problem raised in @sec-toward-architecture-world-models.

5. **How should representations preserve failed runs and rejected alternatives?**
Rejected candidates, missed timing constraints, invalid parameters, and proxy
wins that fail at higher fidelity define much of the design-space boundary, but many learned
representations keep mainly successful artifacts. A paper-quality result would
introduce a failure-and-rejection dataset or task suite and test whether models learn
to avoid repeated failures without overgeneralizing one failure into a false
universal rule. The provenance discussion in @sec-provenance-coverage-and-negative-traces and the examples in @tbl-negative-traces define the evidence such representations would need to
preserve.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked what architecture data must record before an AI-assisted
method can act and a reviewer can audit the resulting architectural claim.
Representation is the first hard problem because a method can only act on what
the loop can see. Get it wrong and a more capable model simply moves faster in
the wrong direction. Get it right and the loop gains a versioned account of the
state, action, observation, rejection, and decision, while a runnable receipt can
add the environment and inputs needed for replay.

Legibility is the bar, and it sits higher than readability. A representation that
captures winning results but not rejected alternatives, or scores but not
provenance, hands the loop a biased and unauditable view of the space.

The durable rule is to make architecture work legible to the automated loop,
which means recording paired, versioned, multi-fidelity design trajectories with
provenance, assumptions, coverage, failed runs, and rejected alternatives, not
only successful endpoints. World models add scoped predictions over that
represented history rather than replacing it.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Represent paired design trajectories so state, action, observation, provenance, coverage, rejection, and decision remain connected across versions and fidelity levels.
- **Reader test:** Could an automated method connect a winning or rejected candidate to the state and action that produced it, the observation that judged it, and the decision that followed?
- **Up next:** Representation says what the loop records. @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces defines which actions are legal and which observations the tools can return.
:::
